∞ Image Link: Hide and Seek Room Model

p.1-4 "This is me" - "map of my house."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the pictures on p.2-3. Take turns matching the picture of the real object on p.2 with its symbol on p.3. Find the child's bedroom on p.4 by matching it to the symbol bedroom on p.3.

Model to Symbol Map
⍞ Materials: model of room from lesson 7

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MODELING DAY
Using the model for help, draw a symbol map of the room like in Me on the Map.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Map from Room Model

➜ NOTE
Keep symbol map for next lesson.

Hide & Seek with Symbol Map
⍞ Materials: symbol map of room from the previous lesson, sticker, penny or other small object

PREP: Hide one object in the room. Place a sticker or other marker on the symbol map to show where it is hidden.

➜ RECAP
What model did you make last time?

Build a Model of the Street
⍞ Materials: blocks, Legos, or other objects, optional: tray to build on

➜ RECAP
What did we play last time with your model?

➜ DISCUSS
Do you know your address? If you do not, start learning it. It is good to know where you live. (Classroom teachers, ask parents for addresses so students can work on this.)

➜ MODELING DAY
Use blocks, Legos, or other objects to make a model of your home and immediate neighbors. You will need to go across the street and use blocks to represent your home and neighboring buildings. If weather does not permit, build the view across the street from a window. See picture for an example.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Street Model

➜ NOTE
Keep model for next and other future lessons, or take a picture so you can easily rebuild it.

Hide & Seek with Model of Home and Neighbors
⍞ Materials: model of home and neighbors, object (should be large enough to find)

PREP: Teacher hide one object near home. Place a sticker or other marker on the model to show where it is hidden.

➜ RECAP
What model did you make last time?

➜ PLAY
Have student look at the model to determine where the object is hidden and then go find it. Take turns hiding and finding objects.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Street Model Hide and Seek

➜ NOTE
Keep model or picture of model for next lesson.

Model to Symbol Street Map
⍞ Materials: Me on the Map, model of home and neighbors from lesson 13, paper, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What did we play last time with your model?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-5 "This is my house" - "map of my street."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the pictures on p.5. Take turns matching the picture of the real object on top of the page with its symbol below it. We only built one side of our street, but we will draw our map as she did in the book.

➜ MODELING DAY
Using the model for help, draw a symbol map of the street like in Me on the Map. If you run out of time, you may finish in the next lesson.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Map from Street Model

➜ NOTE
Keep symbol map for next lesson.

Street Map
⍞ Materials: your street map, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What activity did you do last time?

➜ MODELING DAY
Using the model for help, finish drawing a symbol map of the street like in Me on the Map.

➜ DISCUSS
Have you learned your address yet? If so, tell someone. Keep working if you do not know it yet.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
If you have access to something like Google Maps, you can find the street view of your house. Compare it to your drawing and see if you can locate neighboring buildings.

➜ NOTE
For the next lesson, the student will add the location of sunrise and sunset to their symbol map of the street. Please help them note where the sun rises and sets over the next week for multiple days.

Add Sunrise and Sunset to Street Map
⍞ Materials: your street map, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What activity did you do last time?

➜ DISCUSS
Do you remember learning about sunrise and sunset from the book "What Makes Day and Night" and how the child can see the sunrise from their bed? Today, we are going to add sunrise and sunset to your street map.

➜ DRAW
Add a small drawing of the sun where it rises and sets on your street map. See picture for example.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Sunrise and Sunset on Street Map

The Surface of the Earth, Land vs. Water
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe, world wall map

➜ RECAP
What did you add to your map last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 34 p.107-109 "Wherever we go" - "in the eastern."

➜ DISCUSS
Look at the colors on the globe. What do you think they represent? How does the globe compare to the maps in this lesson?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

assignment_description:
The Surface of the Earth, Continents
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe, world wall map

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 35 p.111-113 "In the eastern" - "with their size."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the globe. Point to some water. Point to some land. Let's find North America together. Find the "belt" of the globe. This is called the equator.

Matching Maps with the Globe and Wall Map
⍞ Materials: globe, world wall map

➜ NOTE
The object is to build understanding and familiarity between a 3 and 2-dimensional map of Earth. The teacher points to something on one map, and the student locates the same place on the other map. Alternate starting a match with either the globe or the world wall map.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ PLAY
Play with a globe and a 2-dimensional world map. Some example questions:
• Locate North America on the world map. (Ask while the teacher is pointing to it on the globe.)
• Let's find Antarctica, where penguins live.
• Continue with other land masses, as well as oceans.

I Spy with the Globe and Wall Map
⍞ Materials: globe, world wall map

➜ NOTE
The object is twofold. First, there is practice learning and finding the continents. Second, there is continued practice using both a 3 and 2-dimensional map of the Earth. Ask the "I Spy" question and allow the student to find it on one of the maps, while you find it on the other. Then compare your answers, making sure to alternate regularly between the globe and the map.

➜ RECAP
What activity did you do last time?

➜ PLAY
Example Questions:
• "I spy a continent that is oval-shaped up top and skinnier on the bottom." (Africa)
• "I spy a continent near the bottom of the globe that has water all around it." (Antarctica)
• "I spy a continent that is connected to another one by a narrow strip of land." (North or South America)
• "I spy an ocean that spans between North America and Europe." (Atlantic)
• "I spy an ocean that does not touch the equator." (Arctic)

Demonstration
⍞ Materials: globe, 2-dimensional world map, an orange, sharp knife, spoon, one inflated balloon, marker, scissors

➜ RECAP
What game did we play last time?

➜ DISCUSS
Compare the globe to the orange and the balloon. Are they similar? We are going to try to make the orange and the balloon flat.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Cut the orange in half, from top to bottom, and scoop out the flesh. Are you able to flatten it? If so, how?
2\. Teacher draws simple shapes of the continents on the blown-up balloon. Carefully cut it from pole to pole. Try to stretch it flat. Can you see how the shapes change near the edges? What happens to them?
3\. Compare your orange and balloon to the 2-dimensional world map.
4\. Which map, a globe or a 2-dimensional map, is more accurate? Why?

Sight-Seeing

➜ NOTE
When the student created the street map, they had to pay attention to the placement of objects. Today, the student will add to that the ability to verbally describe their street. Charlotte Mason described this as sight-seeing in her writings.

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ MEMORY EXERCISE
Have the student go outside (or look out different windows) and look closely at a building near your home. The teacher is not allowed to look. Then, the student describes the building in as much detail as possible, and the teacher has to guess which building it is. Student and teacher can take turns describing different homes/buildings near them.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Elementary Geography
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 1 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Elementary Geography: Tell what happens when you try to flatten out something that is round, like the Earth or an orange. (How does the Earth’s roundness make it hard to draw a flat map?)

· Me on the Map: Build a model of a room in your home.

Poles and Axis
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

assignment_description:
Hemispheres
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 11 p.34-35 "As the ends" - "southern hemisphere."

➜ DISCUSS
⍞ Map Resource: Globe
Look at the globe. Locate the equator. Point to the Northern Hemisphere. Now point to the Southern Hemisphere. In which hemisphere do we live?

What Makes Seasons?
⍞ Materials: The Reasons for Seasons, globe

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-7 "Spring" - "other hemisphere."

➜ DEMONSTRATE
⍞ Map Resource: Globe
Look at the globe. Locate the equator and the two poles. Imagine a straight line going from pole to pole and all the way through. Your globe may have one of these. Have the student be the sun and stand in the middle of the room. As the teacher walks the "earth" around the "sun," recreate and narrate what was happening on p.6-7.

Spring
⍞ Materials: The Reasons for Seasons

➜ NOTE
The next 4 lessons are about the seasons. As an option, you can begin each lesson with a short seasonal poem.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-12 "In the Northern" - "periods of time."

Summer
⍞ Materials: The Reasons for Seasons

➜ NOTE
You can begin with a short seasonal poem.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

p.13-17 "Summer begins" - "nights are shorter."

p.18-22 "Autumn begins" - "autumn sky."

assignment_description:
Seasons at the Equator and Poles
⍞ Materials: The Reasons for Seasons

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about the seasons? I wonder if everyone's seasons are the same.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.28-31 "Areas near" - "repeat themselves."

Our World is a Planet
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 6 p.17-19 "About three hundred" - "of the sun."

➜ NOTE
On p.18, the book states, "We cannot get out of our own world." This is because the book was written in 1881.

Other Planets
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 7 p.21-22 "The great sun" - "He has given them."

Other Planets cont'd
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ INTRO
Would it take longer to walk around a table or to walk around your home/school? Why? (It's farther.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 7 p.22-23 "The eight planets" - "about these yet."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the image. Which planet has the shortest distance? Which one has the longest?
∞ Image Link: Eight Planets Orbiting the Sun

Pictorial and Panoramic Maps
⍞ Materials: paper, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ INTRO
Maps can be drawn in many different ways. Today, your child will be introduced to two views: pictorial and panoramic.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Me on the Map had both pictorial and panoramic view maps. The pictorial map looks like you are right in front of the houses, but the panoramic map looks like you may be on a hill looking across and down at the streets.

➜ MODELING DAY
You will practice this type of mapping by drawing a map of your home or school. Imagine you are on a hill overlooking your home/school. Try to do a panoramic map. (Note: If this is too difficult, use a pictorial map.) Next week, we will draw a map of the yard.

➜ VIEW
See an example of each type of map.
∞ Image Link: Pictorial and Panoramic

Building a Yard Map
⍞ Materials: paper, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ NOTE
This project requires an outdoor open area outside of your home or school. It does not have to be big, but it needs to have things in it to add to a map. It could be your backyard, school playground, garden, etc.

➜ RECAP
What kind of map did you draw last time?

➜ MODELING DAY
You will begin drawing a map of your yard at home or school. You will have two days to complete your map. You can use blocks to begin with if that helps. Imagine you are on a hill overlooking the yard, and draw the area. This can be a panoramic map. (Note: If this is too difficult, use a pictorial map.)

Building a Yard Map cont'd
⍞ Materials: pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What kind of map did you draw last time?

➜ MODELING DAY
Finish drawing the map of your yard at home or school.

➜ NOTE
Save the map for the next lesson.

Going on a Hunt
⍞ Materials: your yard map, treasure of choice

➜ NOTE
This project can be as simple or complicated as you like. You can hide something in the yard and mark it on the map, or hide clues that send them to different parts of the map and ultimately to a "treasure." The teacher could do the simple version, and have the student create a more elaborate plan with clues or multiple things to find for other classmates, siblings, or parents. SAVE the map for future lessons.

➜ RECAP
What kind of map did you draw last time?

➜ PLAY
The teacher uses the map made in the last lesson to hide a treasure in the yard. The teacher marks it on the map, and the student goes hunting for the treasure.

Sunrise and Sunset
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, your yard map, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What activity did you do last time?

➜ INTRO
You might remember where the sun rises at your home or school. If not, get up early and find it.

➜ SET UP
The sun rises in the east, so find some way to mark that direction on the map you drew of your yard.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 24 p.69-71 "One change" - "like to know."

➜ SET UP
Now that you know east, based on your readings, find west, north, and south in the area you mapped. You will add this to your map next week.

Adding Direction to a Map
⍞ Materials: your yard map, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons, your home and neighborhood map (optional)

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ DRAW
Based on your findings in the last lesson, add E (for east), W (for west), N (for north), and S (for south) to your yard map.
If you have your home and neighborhood map, you may add it to that one also. (Just look for your sunrise and sunset marks, and put an E next to the sunrise, etc.)

Creating a Compass Rose
⍞ Materials: a paved area, chalk, compass

➜ RECAP
What did you add to your map last time?

➜ DRAW
1\. Using chalk, draw a large circle on the pavement.
2\. Place the compass in the center.
3\. Once the needle is still, slowly turn the compass until it is pointing north, which is when the needle and mark for north line up.
4\. Next, draw a line from the compass at the north mark to the edge of the circle. Here, write N with the chalk.
5\. Using the compass as a guide, draw lines from the S, W, and E marks of the compass to the edge of the circle, and write the directions, respectively.

You have created a large compass rose.

The Mariner's Compass
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ INTRO
In the last lesson, you used a compass to create a compass rose. Today, we will learn how a compass works.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 29 p.85-87 "Why does the" - "unknown dangers."
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

assignment_description:
Cardinal Direction Game
⍞ Materials: slips of paper, compass, treat

PREP: Find a place outside with enough space to pace off steps for the game. Each slip of paper will have a cardinal direction and a number of paces written upon it. The student will use the magnetic compass to turn toward the appropriate cardinal direction, count off the number of paces, and hunt at that location for the next written directions. Select a place of beginning, a minimum of 3 locations to hide the slips of paper, and a final destination where some sort of treat/reward is hidden.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ PLAY
1\. Have the student stand at the starting point so that the compass is facing N.
2\. Provide the student with written directions for finding the next written directions.
3\. Use the compass to determine which direction to walk, and count off the appropriate number of paces. (Teacher help as needed.)
4\. Hunt for the piece of paper, and continue the journey.
5\. The student’s exploration has ended once the treat is discovered.

Compass Activity
⍞ Materials: compass

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ PLAY
1\. Go outside if possible.
2\. Have student hold compass and face north. While facing north, point west.
3\. Face south, and point east.
4\. What \_\_\_\_ (building, area, etc) is north of you?
5\. Face your home/school. What direction is it (from you)?
6\. Continue in this manner. (If not able to go outside, ask questions based on indoor rooms.)

Boundaries
⍞ Materials: maps you have drawn, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons (optional)

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ DISCUSS
• Does your room have boundaries? Walls, rugs, and curtains are examples.
• Does your home have borders?
• If you have a yard, does it have borders?
• Look back at the maps you have drawn this year, and tell what the boundaries are of each one. (It could be things like walls, streets, lawns, etc.)

➜ DRAW
If your maps do not have boundaries, think about adding some.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Elementary Geography

· Elementary Geography: Explain how to find directions outside and how to add a compass rose to a map.

· The Reason for Seasons: Explain or act out what makes the seasons.

assignment_description:
Highlands and Lowlands
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, kinetic sand

➜ NOTE
Over this term, time will be spent on rain and the water cycle, as well as how geography affects the flow of water. Use opportunities when it rains to observe the flow of water outside, noting which directions it travels, what it must go around, etc.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 36 p.117-118 "Most likely" - "ridges at the top."

➜ MODEL
Build what you think the mountains and mountain chains, with valleys between them, look like from your reading today.

Highlands and Lowlands cont'd
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, kinetic sand

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 36 p.118-119 "After getting up" - "all over the world."

➜ MODEL
Build what you think the mountains and tablelands look like from your reading today.

Model Day
⍞ Materials: Model Magic, pan or tray that will hold water

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MODEL
Build what you think the mountains, mountain chains, tablelands, and valleys look like, but this time in clay. Let it dry for the next lesson.

Model Day
⍞ Materials: model in a pan or tray that will hold water, water source

➜ RECAP
What kind of model did you make last time?

➜ MODEL
Get the model from the last lesson. Choose a high spot and allow the water to pour very slowly over it. Where does the water flow? Try other spots to see if the results are the same or different.

➜ DISCUSS
What did you observe? What conclusions did you draw?

Rain

➜ NOTE
Don't forget to use opportunities when it rains to observe the flow of water outside, noting which directions it travels, what it must go around, etc.

➜ RECAP
What did we discuss last time?

➜ DISCUSS
Have you seen water flowing from your home as a result of rain? Where did it go? Did it flow down? Spend a few minutes talking about your observations. Draw a picture of what you saw, if that is helpful.

➜ DISCUSS
• Did you notice that water is always flowing, sometimes as a trickle, sometimes as a rush?
• It can start as a trickle in a yard, join a flow in a street gutter, join a rushing stream, river, etc.
• Sometimes it stops completely or for a bit, collecting in a puddle, pond, or lake, but sometimes it keeps flowing out on the other end.
• One thing that I hope you noticed was that it was always going to a lower place.

Water Cycle, Rain to Sea
⍞ Materials: Down Comes the Rain

➜ RECAP
What did we discuss last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-17 "Rain comes" - "droplets make clouds."
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

assignment_description:
Moving Water, Going Down
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography

➜ NOTE
This chapter goes into much more depth than Down Comes the Rain, but the foundation of the last lesson is helpful in picturing it and understanding the downward flow of the water cycle.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 37 p.121-124 "Have you ever - "waters flow fast."

Model Day
⍞ Materials: kinetic sand

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MODEL
Spend time recreating the things you read about in the last lesson. (a river with low banks, a river with high banks, a lake with hills surrounding it, a delta)

➜ DISCUSS
Discuss as you build, wondering together what a low versus a high bank would do, a deep valley versus a shallow valley, etc. Be careful not to supply answers; let the student draw their own conclusions.

Water Cycle, Sea to Rain
⍞ Materials: Down Comes the Rain

➜ RECAP
What kind of model did you make last time?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.18-32 "When clouds are" - "it is raining."

Water Vapor
⍞ Materials: The Fairy-land of Science, pot or tea kettle

PREP: Reading today is from an old book. The language will be a little unfamiliar, so it could be good to pre-read, but the descriptions are more in-depth and will hopefully lead to a better understanding.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ DEMONSTRATION
Fill the pot or tea kettle with water. Heat it over the eye of the stove. Wait until the steam begins to rise. Explain that this steam is water that has been heated up and is rising into the air. We don't see it after a while, but it is still there, spreading out into the air. This steam is like the mist you read about in Down Comes the Rain. But, in the ocean, where does the heat come from? It is not from a stove or fire below. (The sun).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.76-81 "Now, although" - "have the same effect."
∞ Book Link: Fairy-land of Science

Make a Simple Rain Gauge
⍞ Materials: mason jar (wide mouth is best), ruler

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ SET UP
1\. Place your mason jar outside in an open spot.
2\. Every time it rains, check the rain by placing the ruler in the jar to measure the height of the water.
3\. Record your observation.
4\. Empty the jar after observation so that you can check it again the next time it rains.
5\. Feel free to do this several times and compare how much it rained each time.

Mapping Routes
⍞ Materials: paper, pencil/colored pencils/markers/crayons

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ DISCUSS
Discuss with your child that they will be making a map of a route they do very regularly over the next several lessons. This could be a ride or walk to school, the grocery store, a park, someone's home, etc.

➜ DRAW
• Draw a picture of each end of your route (for example, your home, your local grocery store).
• Draw yourself going there (for example: riding in a car or walking on a sidewalk).
• Draw several places from memory that you see on the way.
• Keep these drawings for the next lesson.

Mapping Routes cont'd
⍞ Materials: long art paper or paper taped together for the route map, glue or tape, scissors

➜ RECAP
What kind of map did you make last time?

➜ DRAW
• Gather drawings from the last lesson.
• Offer the opportunity to draw something new if they want to.

➜ ACTIVITY
• Cut out the drawings and lay them out in order from the beginning to the end of your route.
• Glue or tape your beginning and ending pictures on opposite ends of the paper.
• Thinking about how you travel, draw roads/sidewalks, and attach each new picture as you come to it.
• Save the map for the next lesson.

Mapping Routes cont'd
⍞ Materials: route map from last lesson

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ NARRATE
Using the map from the last lesson, narrate to someone about your route, using the map you drew to help describe it.

➜ ACTIVITY
Take the map with you on your route, noting what things you had on the map and what things you might add later.

I Spy Game
⍞ Materials: globe, world wall map

➜ RECAP
What did we discuss last time?

➜ PLAY
Using a globe, wall map, or both, play these games, taking turns, to grow familiarity with continents and oceans.

I Spy a Continent
• “I spy on the world map a continent that is oval-shaped up top and skinnier at the bottom.”
• “I spy on the globe a continent near the bottom of the globe that has water all around it.” Use descriptive language.

I Spy an Ocean
• With the globe or world wall map in view, take turns saying, “I spy an ocean that...” Here are some starters:
• ...spans between North America and Europe. (Atlantic)
• ...touches North America, Europe, and Asia. (Arctic)
• ...touches more of the Equator than the other oceans. (Pacific)
• ...does not touch the Equator. (Arctic)

The Oceans and Their Parts
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe or world wall map, kinetic sand

➜ RECAP
What activity did we do last time?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Arctic Ocean.
2\. Locate the ocean surrounding Antarctica.
3\. Locate the Pacific Ocean.
4\. Locate where the Pacific Ocean and the Arctic Ocean meet, between Russia and Alaska. What strait connects the Pacific and Arctic Oceans? (Bering Strait)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 41 p.141-142 "The ocean waters" - "called a strait."

➜ MODEL
Based on your reading, build a strait with the sand.

The Oceans and Their Parts cont'd
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe or world wall map, kinetic sand

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Pacific Ocean.
2\. Locate 5 islands in the Pacific Ocean. (many to choose from)
3\. On the west coast of North America, there is a long, narrow opening where the ocean "goes into" the land. What gulf is this? (Gulf of California)
4\. On the southeast edge of the United States, locate another gulf. (Gulf of Mexico)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 41 p.142-143 "The high parts" - "called a gulf."

Based on your reading, build an island, a group of islands, and a gulf.

The Oceans and Their Parts cont'd
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe or world wall map

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Pacific Ocean.
2\. Locate the continent of Asia.
3\. Locate 3 islands in the Pacific Ocean, along the eastern edge of Asia. (several to choose from)
4\. On the east of Asia, locate at least 3 seas. (Sea of Japan, South China Sea, East China Sea, Yellow Sea, Philippine Sea, Sea of Okhotsk) What do you notice about these seas? (They are connected to the Pacific, but separated slightly by islands.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 41 p.143 "It has made" - "east of Asia."

➜ MAP WORK
Spend a few minutes locating other seas around the globe.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

assignment_description:
The Oceans and Their Parts cont'd
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe or world wall map

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Atlantic Ocean. How does it compare in size to the Pacific? (smaller)
2\. The Pacific has many islands scattered throughout. What do you notice in regard to islands in the Atlantic? (They are not scattered, but some are large.)
3\. Where in the Atlantic are most islands located? (wanting the student to notice that many are very close to the mainland)
4\. Locate a large island in the Atlantic Ocean. (The largest island is Greenland, which extends from the Arctic Ocean into the North Atlantic Ocean. Other islands are listed in the next question.)
5\. Can you locate any more islands in the Atlantic Ocean? (Possibilities include Iceland, Ireland, Great Britain, Newfoundland, and the Falklands, among others.)
6\. Some islands in the Pacific Ocean are isolated, while others exist in groups. Can you locate a group of islands in the Pacific Ocean? (Possibilities include the Aleutian Islands, Hawaii, Galápagos Islands, Japan, Philippine Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, Marshall Islands, Micronesia, Solomon Islands, Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and New Zealand, among many others.)
7\. Can you locate standalone islands in the Pacific that are not part of a group? (This question is more of a scavenger hunt, and the student may only name Vancouver Island and Taiwan. Searching for individual islands in the Pacific Ocean makes it apparent that almost all islands in this ocean are part of a larger group.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lesson 41 p.143-144 "The Atlantic Ocean" - "hottest of the oceans."

➜ MAP WORK
In the northeast of Canada, find the wide body of water that flows into the land. (Hudson Bay) Spend a few minutes locating other bays around the globe.

Map Drill
⍞ Materials: Elementary Geography, globe

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ NOTE
Today will be spent doing a little map drill on the globe. The questions will be based on the previous lessons.

➜ READ
Map Questions p.147-148 #1-11
(Feel free to add your own questions to the drill or have your student come up with some questions.)

➜ NOTE
Next week, set aside time for two geography walks. Look at the lesson plans so you can plan these, either next week or exam week.

Geography Walk

➜ TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
This week, set aside time for two geography walks.

Walk 1- Find moving water and follow it. If it is raining, you can put on rain gear and follow water from around your home. Note where it pools, where it runs quickly, slowly, how it drains away from your house, and where it flows from there. (If it is not a time of year when it rains, possibly use a hose to see where the water would go if it were raining.)

Geography Walk

➜ TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
This week, time should have been set aside for two geography walks.

Walk 2- Find a pond or lake and walk around it to find its source and possibly its "exit." If you cannot find a source, could it be a spring?
